why: B gives the correct check-cert command plus accurate delete/import tmsh syntax and the right K-article link, more complete and faithful than A's GUI-heavy and C's loose syntax.
All three correctly recognize the context lacks ASM-cookie info and honestly refuse to fabricate; C is marginally clearest about what the provided articles actually cover.
A accurately describes OneConnect, its mechanics, NTLM and HA mirroring caveats with correct citations; C is also strong and concise but A is the most complete while staying grounded.
A's ACCESS::session get pattern in ACCESS_REQUEST is closest to real APM iRule usage, while B invents ACCESS::session.get/.exists dot-syntax that is not valid F5 iRule syntax.

All three fabricate an FQDN/config-sync/Bug-1297257 topic when K000140241 is actually about pool member status changing to Unknown after monitor changes; A is least bad by not appending a fabricated source link.

A and B give plausible GUI/CLI/iControl workflows from real knowledge; A is slightly cleaner and avoids C's invented ssl-cipher-list/default-cert syntax padding, though none cite the canonical check-cert command.

All three give reasonable, accurate closed-book explanations of why a tampered ASM cookie (session/integrity/policy mismatch) breaks after upgrade; A is the most precise without overclaiming specifics.

B most accurately captures OneConnect connection pooling/reuse and the right use cases (high-traffic web/REST, latency-sensitive), while A overstates it as TCP-layer-only and C is vaguer about HTTP reuse.

C uses the correct ACCESS::session data get form with a realistic ACCESS_POLICY_ALLOWED event and concrete vpn: session variable names, edging out A/B which use less standard -key/-scope argument styles.
A and C honestly admit they cannot recall the specific K-article and defer to F5 support (correct closed-book behavior), while B does the same but degrades into garbled mixed-language text.
